skill.name: gke-expert
skill.description: Expert guidance for Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) operations including cluster management, workload deployment, scaling, monitoring, troubleshooting, and optimization. Use when working with GKE clusters, Kubernetes deployments on GCP, container orchestration, or when users need help with kubectl commands, GKE networking, autoscaling, workload identity, or GKE-specific features like Autopilot, Binary Authorization, or Config Sync.
skill.summary: Guide GKE cluster operations, deployment patterns, troubleshooting, scaling, and production hardening.
skill.author: AdminTurnedDevOps

security_audit.summary: Static weak-cryptography alerts are false positives caused by ordinary GKE, Kubernetes, and template wording; no crypto APIs or weak hash use were found. The skill includes user-facing kubectl and gcloud commands, including an interactive exec example, so publication is acceptable with an operational-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Operational Commands Can Affect Live GKE Clusters
medium_findings.description: The skill provides kubectl and gcloud command examples for cluster creation, deployment, scaling, debugging, cleanup, and interactive pod shell access. These are legitimate for a GKE operations skill, but misuse against production clusters could cause service impact or expose pod internals.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and relevant to GKE administration. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the skill only documents commands and does not execute them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns in SKILL.md and scripts/hello_world.py, but the referenced lines contain GKE guidance, Kubernetes terms, plain documentation, or argparse description text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines were inspected directly and contain no cryptographic imports, functions, or hash configuration. The matches appear to be substring or keyword collisions in ordinary documentation.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point To Official Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings reference Google Cloud and Kubernetes documentation links. These links do not collect data, perform callbacks, or direct users to unknown endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs are visible documentation references for GKE, kubectl, Workload Identity, best practices, or Google Cloud pricing. No network code or exfiltration behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interactive Pod Shell Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests kubectl exec into a pod shell. This is standard troubleshooting guidance, but users should apply normal access controls and avoid running it against sensitive workloads without authorization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present and can expose a pod shell. It is also a normal Kubernetes debugging action, so the concern depends on user permissions and environment.

content.user_title: Manage GKE Clusters With Expert Guidance
content.value_statement: GKE teams often need quick, accurate help across cluster setup, deployment, scaling, and troubleshooting. This skill provides structured Google Kubernetes Engine guidance with practical kubectl and gcloud examples.
actual_capabilities: Guides Autopilot and Standard GKE cluster creation workflows
Provides deployment, service, ingress, and pod disruption budget examples
Explains HPA, cluster autoscaling, VPA recommendations, and Spot VM usage
Supports troubleshooting for pods, services, resources, events, and connectivity
Covers Workload Identity setup and basic production security context patterns
Links users to official GKE, kubectl, and Google Cloud documentation
limitations: Does not connect to Google Cloud or inspect live cluster state
Cannot validate account permissions, quotas, regions, or project configuration
Examples require review before use in production environments
Does not replace official Google Cloud support for outages or incidents

use_cases.title: Plan A New GKE Cluster
use_cases.description: Choose Autopilot or Standard mode, size initial capacity, and prepare authentication steps.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot A Failing Workload
use_cases.description: Walk through pod events, logs, service endpoints, resource pressure, and common Kubernetes failure states.

use_cases.title: Harden A Production Deployment
use_cases.description: Review workload identity, HTTPS ingress, disruption budgets, security contexts, and autoscaling practices.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Cluster Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between GKE Autopilot and Standard for a new service. Ask for the missing workload, budget, and operations details.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy An Application
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GKE deployment plan for my container image, service exposure, resource requests, and rollout validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Debug A Pod Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through troubleshooting a GKE pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff. Include commands, likely causes, and safe rollback options.

prompt_templates.title: Design Production Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my GKE production design for autoscaling, Workload Identity, ingress, security context, disruption budgets, and monitoring gaps.

output_examples.input: My pod is pending in GKE. What should I check?
output: Start with pod events and node capacity.
Check quotas, node selectors, taints, and resource requests.
Compare requested CPU and memory with available node resources.
Review cluster autoscaler status if the cluster should add nodes.

output_examples.input: How should I expose a web service on GKE?
output: Use a Kubernetes Service for stable internal routing.
Add an Ingress when you need HTTP routing and managed certificates.
Confirm health checks, backend service status, and firewall behavior.
Review production security before exposing public traffic.
Prefer Autopilot when automatic right-sizing fits the workload.
Set realistic resource requests and limits.
Use Spot VMs only for fault-tolerant workloads.
Review autoscaling bounds to avoid runaway capacity.

best_practices: Confirm project, region, cluster type, and environment before applying commands.
Test manifest and scaling changes in non-production clusters first.
Use least-privilege IAM and Workload Identity for application access.
anti_patterns: Running destructive cleanup commands without checking the active context.
Using broad service account permissions for every workload.
Copying sample resource limits directly into production without load data.

faq.question: Can this skill execute commands on my cluster?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. You must run any kubectl or gcloud commands yourself.

faq.question: Does it support both GKE Autopilot and Standard?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for both modes and notes where Standard-only node management applies.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot live incidents?
faq.answer: It can guide diagnosis steps, but it cannot inspect your cluster or replace incident response procedures.

faq.question: Does it cover Workload Identity?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes setup steps for linking Kubernetes and Google Cloud service accounts.

faq.question: Is this safe for production use?
faq.answer: Use it as guidance only. Review commands, contexts, permissions, and change windows before production changes.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
